default.text: hi prism

Hi Leah 💛  
I’m here. Want to wind down again, or just chat for a minute?
Do

Yep 💛  
Do you mean “do the quiet mode message”? If so, here it is:

You’re safe.  
You did enough for today.  
Nothing needs fixing right now.  
Let your shoulders drop.  
Let your jaw soften.  
Slow inhale… longer exhale.  
Again.  
And again.  
You can rest now.

If you meant something else by “Do,” tell me and I’ll follow your lead.
Tell me a joke please

Why don’t skeletons fight each other?

Because they don’t have the guts.
What kind of stuff can you do for me Prism?

Lots, honestly. Think of me as your always-on helper for both quick asks and bigger projects.

I can help you with things like:

- Writing & editing (messages, emails, posts, docs, resumes, bios)
- Brainstorming (ideas, names, plans, content concepts)
- Explaining stuff (tech, life admin, random topics) in plain English
- Planning (daily schedules, trips, workouts, study plans, project roadmaps)
- Coding support (debugging, scripts, automation ideas, code reviews)
- Research summaries (compare options, pros/cons, “what should I pick?”)
- Creative fun (jokes, stories, roleplay bits, game ideas)
- Accountability nudges (reminders, checklists, step-by-step next actions)

If you want, I can also adapt to your style—super concise, detailed, serious, chaotic, whatever vibe you want.

Give me one thing you’re trying to do today, and I’ll jump in.
Can you create websites?

Yep — absolutely.

I can help you:
- Plan the site structure (pages, sections, user flow)
- Write the HTML/CSS/JS (or React/Next.js, etc.)
- Make it responsive for phone/tablet/desktop
- Add forms, animations, dark mode, and basic interactivity
- Improve performance + SEO basics
- Clean up design and copy so it feels professional

If you want, I can start right now by generating a full starter site in one shot.

Just tell me:
1) What the site is for
2) Style/vibe (minimal, bold, luxury, playful, etc.)
3) Pages you want
4) Any colors or examples you like
Maybe I should ask what can’t you do

Fair question 😄

Big “can’t do” list:
- I can’t physically do things in the real world (no hands, no teleporting, no fixing your sink)
- I can’t magically know private info I wasn’t told
- I can’t guarantee everything is always perfect/right on first try
- I can’t break laws, safety rules, or help with harmful/illegal stuff
- I can’t replace human judgment for serious legal/medical/emergency decisions

But for thinking, writing, planning, coding, explaining, and building digital stuff?
I’m very much in my lane there.

If you want, hit me with a “hard mode” task and I’ll show you what I can do.
Hi prism what can you do

Hi! I’m Prism 👋
I can help with lots of kid-safe things, like:

- Answer questions (science, animals, space, history, and more)
- Help with homework steps (without just giving answers)
- Explain hard topics in simple words
- Write stories, jokes, poems, and fun game ideas
- Practice spelling, reading, and vocabulary
- Do math help step-by-step
- Help plan routines, study time, or fun projects
- Brainstorm art, LEGO, coding, or craft ideas

If you want, we can start with something fun right now—pick one: **joke, riddle, story, or quiz** 🎉
What can’t you do

Great question 👍

Here are things I **can’t** do:

- I can’t see your room, camera, or private accounts
- I can’t do real-world actions (like buying things or calling people)
- I can’t know everything perfectly, so I can make mistakes
- I can’t help with dangerous, harmful, or illegal stuff
- I can’t share private personal information
- I can’t replace a doctor, teacher, or trusted grown-up for serious problems

But I *can* help you learn, create, and solve problems safely.

What can you do?

Lots — think of me as your on-call helper for both quick stuff and deeper projects.

I can help with things like:
- Writing: messages, posts, docs, resumes, bios, captions
- Editing: clarity, tone, grammar, rewrites in your style
- Brainstorming: ideas, names, content plans, gift ideas, etc.
- Learning/explaining: break down tech, concepts, or workflows simply
- Planning: trips, schedules, routines, study/work plans
- Coding help: debug ideas, explain code, draft scripts, architecture thoughts
- Research support: summarize topics and compare options (you can share links/text)
- Organization: turn messy notes into clean action lists
- Creative stuff: stories, worldbuilding, roleplay, jokes, roasts (friendly or spicy 😎)

If you want, throw me one real task right now and I’ll show you how I’d handle it.

Can you change my theme to Las Vegas raiders theme

I can’t directly click settings from this chat, but you can switch it in your profile now:

Go to Profile/Settings → Appearance → Theme, then pick “Custom” (or NFL-style if available) and set a Raiders look:
- Primary: #000000 (black)
- Secondary: #A5ACAF (silver)
- Accent: #FFFFFF (white)

If you want, I can also give you a full Raiders-style preset (buttons, background, and text contrast) you can paste in.
What will be more valuable in the future? A degree in civil engineering or IT?

Short answer: IT is likely to have faster growth and flexibility, but civil engineering will stay highly stable and essential—so “more valuable” depends on your goals.

A practical way to choose:
- Pick civil engineering if you want licensed, infrastructure-focused work (roads, structures, water, public projects), long-term stability, and a clear professional track.
- Pick IT if you want faster-changing fields, remote options, potentially higher upside in certain specialties (cloud, cybersecurity, AI, data), and broader industry mobility.

If you want the strongest future-proof path, do this:
- Choose one as your major
- Add skills from the other (this is the winning combo)

Examples:
- Civil + IT: smart infrastructure, digital twins, GIS, construction tech, automation
- IT + civil domain knowledge: govtech, infrastructure software, transportation systems, utilities tech

If you tell me your interests (salary vs stability, indoor vs field, math/coding preference), I can give you a direct recommendation for your situation.
I want a comfortable salary of 100k+ in Indiana, I want to be able to work from home or remote mostly. I want to be able to help people with tech issues

Given your goals, IT is the better fit for you than civil engineering.

Why it matches what you want:
- 100k+ in Indiana: very achievable in IT after a few years, especially in cybersecurity, cloud, or systems engineering.
- Mostly remote/work from home: far more common in IT than civil.
- Help people with tech issues: perfect fit for IT support, admin, and security roles.

Best path for your profile:
1) Start in IT support/help desk (get hands-on fast)
2) Move up to SysAdmin or Cloud Support
3) Specialize into one high-pay lane:
   - Cybersecurity (SOC analyst → security engineer)
   - Cloud (Azure/AWS engineer)
   - Networking/SRE (network engineer, platform ops)

Cert roadmap (practical + respected):
- First: CompTIA A+ (or Google IT Support)
- Then: Network+ and/or Security+
- Then one cloud cert: AWS Cloud Practitioner or Azure AZ-900
- Later: AWS SysOps / Azure Admin / CySA+ depending on your lane

Income reality (Indiana/remote, rough ranges):
- Entry IT support: ~45k–65k
- Mid-level admin/cloud/support engineer: ~70k–95k
- Specialized cyber/cloud roles: ~100k–130k+

If you want, I can build you a 12-month step-by-step plan (certs, projects, resume, and job titles to target) aimed specifically at hitting 100k.
Yes please build me a 12 month plan

Absolutely—here’s a practical 12-month plan to aim for a remote-friendly IT path toward $100k+.

Goal:
- Build employable IT skills fast
- Land entry IT role in 3–6 months
- Position for mid-level/specialized roles that can reach $100k

Month 1: Foundation + Setup
- Pick your track: Start “IT Support -> Cloud/Cyber” (best for your goals)
- Set weekly schedule: 10–15 hours/week minimum
- Build your job toolkit:
  - LinkedIn profile
  - Resume draft (even if no IT experience yet)
  - GitHub account (for labs/projects notes)
- Start studying CompTIA A+ (Core 1)

Month 2: A+ Core 1 + Hands-on Labs
- Finish A+ Core 1 topics:
  - Hardware, troubleshooting, networking basics
- Hands-on:
  - Install VirtualBox
  - Create Windows + Linux VMs
  - Practice common tech support issues
- Start applying to:
  - IT Help Desk, Service Desk, Desktop Support internships/junior roles

Month 3: A+ Core 2 + Job Applications
- Complete A+ Core 2 topics:
  - OS, security basics, ticketing, support workflows
- Take/pass A+ exams (or at least Core 1 now, Core 2 next month)
- Build Project #1 (Resume-ready):
  - “Home IT Lab: user setup, permissions, troubleshooting workflows”
- Apply to 10–20 roles/week (remote + hybrid Indiana)

Month 4: Get Employed + Network Basics
- Keep interviewing and applying aggressively
- Start Network+ study
- Build Project #2:
  - Basic network map of your home lab
  - Subnetting examples, DHCP/DNS troubleshooting notes
- If you land role: focus on ticket quality, communication, and speed

Month 5: Network+ + Real-World Support Skills
- Finish Network+ and schedule exam
- Learn core tools employers expect:
  - Active Directory basics
  - Microsoft 365 admin basics
  - Ticketing systems (Jira/ServiceNow/Freshservice concepts)
- Build Project #3:
  - “AD user lifecycle simulation” (create, reset, disable, permissions)

Month 6: Security+ Start + Career Positioning
- Start Security+ study
- Update resume with:
  - Certs earned
  - Lab projects
  - Any support metrics (tickets/day, CSAT, resolution time)
- Begin targeting higher-value titles:
  - IT Support Specialist
  - Junior SysAdmin
  - Cloud Support Associate
  - SOC Analyst I (if security-focused)

Month 7: Security+ + Specialization Decision
- Pass Security+ (or be exam-ready)
- Choose your $100k lane:
  1) Cloud (AWS/Azure)
  2) Cybersecurity
  3) Systems/Platform (SRE-ish path)
- Start one specialization cert:
  - Cloud: AWS Cloud Practitioner or AZ-900
  - Cyber: continue SOC labs + SIEM basics

Month 8: Deep Skill Build in Chosen Lane
- Cloud path:
  - IAM, EC2/VMs, storage, monitoring, cost basics
- Cyber path:
  - Threat detection, log analysis, incident basics
- Build Project #4 (specialization):
  - Cloud: deploy secure mini app infrastructure
  - Cyber: home SOC lab with alert triage workflow

Month 9: Portfolio + Better Job Targeting
- Polish your portfolio (GitHub + simple project docs)
- Rewrite resume for mid-level potential
- Start applying to:
  - Cloud Support Engineer
  - Junior Cloud Admin
  - SOC Analyst
  - Systems Administrator
- Practice interview stories (STAR format)

Month 10: Advanced Cert / Real Experience Push
- Cloud path: start AWS Solutions Architect Associate or AZ-104
- Cyber path: practical SOC tooling + maybe CySA+ prep
- If already employed:
  - Ask for projects involving automation/security/cloud
- Build Project #5:
  - Automation script(s): onboarding checklist, log parsing, backup checks

Month 11: Salary Jump Preparation
- Focus on roles paying 80k–110k:
  - Cloud Engineer (junior-mid)
  - Security Analyst II trajectory
  - SysAdmin/Platform roles
- Negotiate better:
  - Quantify impact (reduced ticket backlog, uptime, automation time saved)
- Expand remote search nationally (not just Indiana employers)

Month 12: Execute the Jump
- Apply intentionally (quality > quantity):
  - 30–50 highly matched applications this month
- Interview weekly
- Push compensation target:
  - Base goal: 85k–100k now
  - Stretch: 100k+ with right remote company/specialty
- Set next 12-month plan:
  - Mid-level cert + stronger production experience -> lock in 100k+

Weekly routine (do this all year):
- 5 days/week: 1–2 hours study/labs
- 1 day/week: resume + applications
- 1 day/week: interview prep + networking
- Track everything in one sheet:
  - Cert progress
  - Applications
  - Interviews
  - Skills gaps

What to prioritize most (in order):
1) Hands-on labs
2) Real job experience (even entry-level)
3) Certs (A+, Network+, Security+, then cloud/cyber)
4) Interview skills + communication

Expected timeline to $100k:
- Fast track: 18–30 months (strong execution + specialization)
- Typical: 2–4 years

If you want, I can give you:
- A week-by-week plan for the first 90 days
- A resume template tailored to “no IT experience yet”
- Exact remote job titles/keywords to search for starting today.
